overview: The war acquired an explicit retaliation formula. President Trump declared the US would destroy an Iranian bridge or power plant for every Iranian attack on shipping in the Strait of Hormuz — a tit-for-tat doctrine that puts civilian infrastructure squarely in the crosshairs, atop an 11th straight night of strikes and his standing threat to bomb the buried 'Pickaxe Mountain' nuclear complex near Natanz. An Iranian official countered that Tehran would answer by blacking out the Gulf states hosting US forces. The escalation overshadowed US-mediated ceasefire talks, which Secretary of State Marco Rubio undercut by calling Iran 'not serious about a deal,' lifting Brent crude. Meanwhile Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth's demand for roughly $88bn more still drew bipartisan fury in the Senate.

keyPoints.text: Trump said the US will destroy an Iranian bridge or power plant for each Iranian attack in the Strait of Hormuz; an Iranian official warned Tehran would answer by blacking out US-host Gulf states.
Trump's standing threat to hit 'Pickaxe Mountain,' an Iranian nuclear site near Natanz buried ~100m underground, 'very heavily' keeps the war's nuclear dimension live on an 11th night of strikes.

Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth told the Senate the war has cost $37.5bn and asked for ~$88bn more ($67bn to restock arms), facing bipartisan fury; Sen. Gary Peters told him 'you, sir, are the failure.'
Secretary of State Marco Rubio said Iran is 'not serious about a deal,' dimming US-mediated ceasefire talks and helping push Brent crude higher as Hormuz stayed a live shooting zone.

Iran again struck Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan — Jordan's army said it intercepted four of six incoming missiles Wednesday; the Houthis extended their blockade to Bab el-Mandeb and Saudi tankers began turning back.
Iran's judiciary warned against 'infiltrators' and announced new executions under a security law passed after last year's 12-day war — a domestic crackdown running in parallel with the external war.

watch.text: Whether Trump acts on either standing threat — the bridge-or-power-plant retaliation formula for Hormuz attacks, or a strike on the deeply buried 'Pickaxe Mountain' nuclear complex — each of which would open a fresh rung of escalation.

Whether the Senate grants Hegseth's ~$88bn request or attaches conditions — the appropriations vote that hands a Congress already hunting for an exit its first concrete lever over a war it never authorized.
Whether US-mediated ceasefire talks survive Rubio's 'not serious about a deal' verdict and an 11th night of strikes, or are overtaken by the nuclear threat and the widening Red Sea front.

stories.title: Trump vows to destroy a bridge or power plant for each Hormuz attack
stories.summary: President Trump declared that the United States would destroy an Iranian bridge or power plant for every Iranian attack on shipping in the Strait of Hormuz, AP, the BBC and Al Jazeera reported — turning the war's central chokepoint standoff into an explicit tit-for-tat formula aimed at Iran's civilian infrastructure. The threat came after the US military completed its eleventh consecutive night of strikes on Iran. An Iranian official responded that Tehran would answer such attacks by sparking power outages in the countries that host US troops, Al Jazeera reported, extending the retaliation logic to the Gulf states ringing Iran. The exchange makes each side's threat conditional on the other's, converting a naval standoff into a mechanical escalation ladder that climbs a rung with every ship struck.
stories.explainer: A per-attack retaliation formula is dangerous precisely because it removes discretion: once a leader publicly ties a specific response to a specific trigger, backing down looks like weakness and following through is automatic, so the fighting escalates without anyone deciding to escalate. Targeting bridges and power plants also shifts the war deeper into civilian infrastructure — dual-use targets whose destruction hits ordinary Iranians and courts a humanitarian and legal backlash — while Iran's counter-threat to black out the Gulf states hosting US bases turns those hosts into hostages of the exchange, raising the pressure on them to demand a ceasefire.

stories.title: Trump's threat to strike 'Pickaxe Mountain' keeps the nuclear file live
stories.summary: President Trump's threat to attack an underground Iranian nuclear facility 'very heavily,' reported by the BBC and Guardian, remained a live danger on the eleventh consecutive night of US strikes — a threat that pulls the war back toward the nuclear file it had partly moved past. The target is the complex known in the West as 'Pickaxe Mountain' (Kuh-e Kolang), sited near the Natanz enrichment plant and estimated to be buried almost 100 metres inside a mountain, making it far harder to destroy than surface sites already hit. Little is publicly known about the facility, but analysts have long assessed it as a deeply protected part of Iran's nuclear infrastructure. The threat lands with IAEA safeguards access suspended since earlier strikes on Natanz and Iran having called off its interim-deal commitments — meaning no inspectors are inside to gauge the consequences of any strike.
stories.explainer: Striking a hardened, deeply buried nuclear site is a different order of escalation from bombing shipping or power plants. A facility 100 metres underground is built to survive conventional bombs, so hitting it 'very heavily' implies the largest bunker-busting ordnance and raises the prospect of either failure or an uncontrolled release near sensitive nuclear material. With inspectors locked out, neither side — nor the outside world — would have an independent reading of what a strike actually did to Iran's most protected nuclear infrastructure, which is precisely what makes the threat so destabilising.

stories.title: Hegseth tells Senate the war has cost $37.5bn — and asks for $88bn more
stories.summary: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth told the Senate that the US-Israel war on Iran has cost $37.5bn so far and asked the appropriations committee for nearly $88bn more to sustain it — including roughly $67bn to restock depleted munitions — the Guardian, Reuters and PBS reported. The request drew open bipartisan hostility: Senator Gary Peters, the Michigan Democrat, told Hegseth 'you, sir, are the failure' over the lack of a coherent Iran strategy, while Hegseth, joined by Joint Chiefs chairman Gen. Dan Caine, insisted Iran had been 'weakened' but refused to give any timeline for the war's end. The hearing turned an abstract cost into a live vote, forcing a Congress already searching for an exit to decide whether to keep paying for a campaign it never authorized.
stories.explainer: Wars run on appropriated dollars, and once the Pentagon must return to Congress mid-war for tens of billions more, the legislature acquires a financial choke-point it otherwise lacks. The $67bn to 'restock arms' is telling: it means the US has been firing off munitions faster than it can replace them, a material limit on how long an air campaign can be sustained. By pairing a war-powers push with control of the purse, lawmakers now hold two levers at once — and Hegseth's refusal to name an end date is exactly what hardens their resolve to attach conditions or refuse.

stories.title: Rubio calls Iran 'not serious about a deal' as oil climbs
stories.summary: Secretary of State Marco Rubio said Iran is 'not serious about a deal,' a comment that helped push oil prices higher and further dimmed the US-mediated ceasefire diplomacy, CNBC reported. The remark hardened the mood around the Strait of Hormuz — through which roughly a fifth of the world's oil moves and which remained a live shooting zone through the day — where the US is striking to force shipping open and Iran is attacking tankers to keep it shut. Brent crude held near its highest of the war and US pump prices stayed above $4 a gallon. Rubio, who days earlier had also warned that an Iranian death toll in Hormuz would set a 'dangerous precedent,' cast the assessment as realism about Tehran's intentions, but it read on the ground as Washington narrowing the diplomatic opening even as mediators keep working.
stories.explainer: A strait where vessels are being hit and abandoned is one insurers and shippers avoid regardless of any official status, so the economic damage compounds with every ship struck. When the top US diplomat publicly judges the other side 'not serious,' it signals to markets that the fighting will continue — which is why the comment moved oil — and it raises the domestic cost for any subsequent US concession. Rubio's framing tightens the loop that keeps crude elevated and the strait closed, even as third-party mediators press on.

stories.title: Iran hits Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan again; Houthis widen the Red Sea front
stories.summary: Iran struck Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan again in reprisal for the US bombings, Al Jazeera reported, continuing the pattern of retaliation falling on the US-aligned Gulf states that ring Iran rather than on American soil directly. Jordan's army said it intercepted four of six Iranian missiles fired Wednesday, with two falling in remote, unpopulated areas, AFP reported. In parallel the Yemeni Houthis extended their maritime campaign: after declaring a blockade of Saudi Arabia they announced a blockade of Bab el-Mandeb — the second strait they had threatened — and Reuters reported that tankers carrying Saudi crude have begun turning back. The overlapping fronts mean the war now presses on two chokepoints and a widening ring of host states at once: Iran raising the cost of sheltering US bases, and its Houthi allies squeezing the Red Sea approaches to the region's largest oil producer.
stories.explainer: Iran fights asymmetrically, so its reprisals land on Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan — the states that host the bases — to make sheltering American forces politically and economically unbearable, while the Houthis open a second maritime front Iran need not staff itself. Bab el-Mandeb is the southern gateway to the Red Sea and Suez; blockading it alongside Hormuz threatens to reroute or halt a large share of Gulf energy exports. Tankers already turning away from Saudi crude is the first concrete sign the threat is reshaping real shipping decisions, not just insurance premiums.

stories.title: Iran's judiciary warns of 'infiltrators,' announces new executions
stories.summary: Iran's judiciary warned against 'infiltrators' and announced new executions, Al Jazeera reported, as the state turned inward under the pressure of the war. Many of the security cases are being prosecuted under a law adopted after last year's 12-day war with Israel — a wartime statute now being applied to a fresh wave of arrests and death sentences. The move underscores that alongside the external war fought across the Gulf and the Strait of Hormuz, Iran's leadership is waging an internal campaign against suspected collaborators and dissent, a domestic front that has widened as the fighting has dragged on.
stories.explainer: A leadership decapitated on the war's first day and forced to operate on the move is acutely vulnerable to infiltration and internal challenge, so a wartime crackdown serves two purposes: it hunts for the informants who could enable more targeted strikes, and it deters any move against the surviving command. Reaching for a security law written after last year's conflict shows Tehran institutionalising wartime repression — which matters for diplomacy, because a state executing suspected 'infiltrators' at home is one hardening its posture, not preparing to concede.

stories.title: Strikes and Rubio's skepticism overshadow ceasefire talks
stories.summary: US-mediated efforts to salvage the collapsed interim ceasefire, which had moved to Pakistan, were overshadowed anew as the eleventh consecutive night of US strikes and Rubio's judgment that Iran is 'not serious about a deal' undercut the diplomacy, AP and the Guardian reported. Mediators had pressed a specific proposal — earlier framed as a 10-day ceasefire — to pause the fighting long enough for both sides to step back from the chokepoints, but the effort is running against a hardening war: Hegseth would set no timeline for its end, Iran has called off its interim-deal commitments, and Trump is threatening Iran's nuclear sites and its infrastructure. Iran's government says diplomatic contacts continue through intermediaries, leaving negotiation the war's thinnest — and still only — off-ramp.
stories.explainer: With the interim deal dead and IAEA inspectors locked out, there is no standing framework to fall back on, so any de-escalation must be rebuilt from scratch through third parties — the role Oman, Qatar and Pakistan are playing. Talks that proceed under an 11th night of bombing, a per-attack retaliation formula and a public US verdict that Tehran is not serious have little leverage: each strike and each hardening statement raises the price of being seen to concede, narrowing the very opening the mediators are working.

stories.title: The Pentagon's funding crunch becomes a formal ask
stories.summary: The Pentagon's warning that it was running short on cash crystallized into a formal request, with coverage framing the ask as roughly $67-70bn to restock arms within a nearly $88bn total request, Al Jazeera reported, as Iran activated air defences across Tehran on the 11th night of US strikes. The department put the war's cost at $37.5bn so far. The disclosure sits atop the site's running estimate of the war's cost to US taxpayers — above $113bn as of June — and turns a budget shortfall into a live congressional decision that lawmakers of both parties used to challenge the war's direction.
stories.explainer: A mid-war funding request is a different kind of pressure than casualties or oil prices: it forces the administration to seek fresh appropriations from a Congress already searching for a way to end the war, handing lawmakers a vote — and with it the power to attach conditions or refuse — over a campaign run largely on presidential authority.

stories.title: New poll shows even Trump voters losing patience with the war
stories.summary: A new poll shows Trump voters losing patience with the Iran war, which Politico described as leaving the president in 'a politically impossible situation,' even as Trump insisted the midterms would not affect his war plan. The finding compounds earlier reporting, by The Economist, that the renewed conflict is the least popular US war since modern polling began — support falling below the low points of the Iraq and Vietnam wars. The erosion within the president's own base is driving events on Capitol Hill: it underpins the search for a way to terminate the war and the hostile reception Hegseth met at the Senate as American troops die and pump prices climb past $4 a gallon.
stories.explainer: Domestic unpopularity is a strategic variable, not just a political one: a president fighting a war the public rejects has less room to escalate and more incentive to seek an exit — but dead soldiers pull the other way, creating pressure to 'finish' the fight rather than be seen abandoning it. When the disaffection reaches the president's own voters, it sharpens that bind with midterms approaching, which is exactly the pressure now voiced openly in the fight over Hegseth's funding request.

stories.title: Iran's foreign minister recounts the strike that killed Khamenei
stories.summary: Iran's foreign minister, Abbas Araghchi, described being pulled from the rubble of a leadership compound after the US-Israeli strike on the first day of the war — the same strike that killed Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei — and said he has kept constantly on the move since, the Guardian reported. The account is a rare first-person window into Iran's decapitation at the war's outset and how its surviving leadership has operated under threat ever since. It lands as Trump threatens Iran's nuclear sites and infrastructure anew, a reminder that the conflict opened with an attempt to remove Iran's top leadership and has never returned to a stable command footing.
stories.explainer: Killing a state's supreme leader on day one is a decapitation strike, and its aftershocks explain much of the war's shape: a leadership that survives by staying mobile is harder to negotiate with, slower to make binding decisions, and more reliant on hardened institutions like the IRGC. Araghchi's account matters less as news than as context — it shows why Iran's responses have been fragmented and its diplomatic signals mixed, and why any ceasefire must be struck with a leadership still operating from the rubble.

stories.title: Nearly 100 US troops wounded; 17 dead across four host states
stories.summary: The Pentagon's acknowledgment that nearly 100 US service members have been wounded over two weeks of escalating strikes — disclosed a day after the New York Times reported dozens of injuries had been withheld — continues to shape the war at home, Time and the Guardian reported. The confirmed US death toll stands at 17, spread across Iraq, Jordan, Kuwait and Bahrain; the military named the Iraq fatality as Sgt Michael Swinton of Fayetteville, North Carolina, killed during a 'controlled detonation' of a downed Iranian drone, and the Wall Street Journal reported that the Iranian missile that killed two troops at Jordan's Muwaffaq Salti Airbase struck US personnel housing.
stories.explainer: The injured, not just the dead, measure a war's true cost — and a count that jumped from 'dozens withheld' to nearly 100 in a day tells the public the toll has been understated. Each named casualty and each detail of a missile reaching troop housing turns an abstract tally into concrete vulnerability, feeding directly into the congressional push now colliding with Hegseth's funding request: if lawmakers conclude the scale of American losses was hidden, the case to rein the campaign in strengthens.

stories.title: 'It needs to end now': a bipartisan search for the exit
stories.summary: Democratic lawmakers are actively searching for options to halt President Trump's renewed attacks on Iran — 'It needs to end now,' with the president accused of having 'lost control of this war' — turning last week's demand to terminate the war into a hunt for a concrete legislative lever, Politico and the Guardian reported. Senator Chris Murphy, the Connecticut Democrat, argued 'Iran knows Trump will have to stop escalation before it does.' The disquiet is not confined to one party: one House Republican said 'we should have finished this a long time ago,' and the pressure sharpened at Hegseth's Senate hearing, where members of both parties challenged the war's cost and direction.
stories.explainer: Under the War Powers Resolution, Congress can force a floor vote to end US involvement in hostilities it never authorized — and 'searching for options' is the procedural step before such a vote is filed. Combined with Hegseth's ~$88bn appropriations request, the legislature is acquiring two levers at once: a war-powers vote and the power of the purse. The bind is that rising US deaths push the president to escalate while pushing Congress to quit — exactly how past American wars have ended.

stories.title: Oil holds above $90 as the shock ripples out to households
stories.summary: Brent crude for September delivery held above $90 a barrel — its highest of the war — after successive nights of US strikes, fresh tanker fires in the Strait of Hormuz and Rubio's comment that Iran is 'not serious about a deal,' the Guardian and CNBC reported. US average gasoline remains above $4 a gallon and diesel above $5, and the shock is visible in distant economies: experts warn the Reserve Bank of Australia is judged twice as likely to raise interest rates as higher fuel prices feed inflation. Even so, analysts note the war has not yet produced a global oil crisis — spare capacity, alternative pipelines and strategic reserves have absorbed the shock — but a second contested waterway at Bab el-Mandeb shrinks that cushion.
stories.explainer: Roughly a fifth of the world's oil moves through Hormuz, so each vessel actually attacked strips the market of its assumption that some crude always slips through. The $4-a-gallon pump price is the transmission belt from a foreign war to inflation and central-bank decisions worldwide, which is why crude holding above $90 is a global macro event, not just a market one — and why a second blockade at Bab el-Mandeb, forcing Saudi tankers to turn back, threatens to erode the buffers that have so far kept the shock contained.

stories.title: Iran calls off the interim deal, reviving the nuclear file
stories.summary: Tehran's formal rupture with Washington — calling off the interim peace agreement after earlier declaring the June memorandum 'suspended' — remains the backdrop to the renewed nuclear escalation, CNBC reported. With IAEA safeguards access suspended since strikes on Natanz and Iran now threatened with attacks on 'Pickaxe Mountain,' there is neither a live agreement nor independent monitoring over Iran's most sensitive facilities. Mediators are pressing a new ceasefire, but Rubio's judgment that Iran is 'not serious about a deal,' with US troops dead and nearly 100 wounded, has frayed the negotiating thread to near nothing.
stories.explainer: The interim deal was the last guardrail over the war's nuclear dimension: mutual commitments that, however frayed, gave both sides something to point to. Formally calling it off — not merely suspending commitments — removed that guardrail just as inspectors were locked out, which is what makes Trump's threat against a buried nuclear complex so fraught: there is no framework left to breach and no monitor left to verify the result.

stories.title: Nuclear dimension lingers at Bushehr
stories.summary: Satellite imagery published last week showed fresh damage inside Bushehr, Iran's largest nuclear power plant, from strikes dated between 7 and 12 July — the window in which the ceasefire collapsed — and Iranian officials have said US strikes also hit an under-construction nuclear plant. With IAEA safeguards access suspended and Iran having called off its interim-deal commitments, outside monitors are left reliant on satellites to gauge the condition of sensitive facilities. Trump's fresh threat against the 'Pickaxe Mountain' complex near Natanz sharpens the concern that the war is edging toward a nuclear-safety crisis.
stories.explainer: Bushehr is a civilian power reactor, not an enrichment site, so the danger near it is radiological rather than proliferation: damage to a fueled reactor's cooling or containment could release contamination across the Gulf regardless of who is blamed. With inspectors locked out, satellite analysis is the only independent window into whether strikes on any Iranian nuclear facility — Bushehr or the buried complex Trump now threatens — are edging toward a safety crisis.

stories.title: Israel says 'no interest' in joining, but readies plans
stories.summary: Israel — which with the United States launched the war's opening blow, Operation Epic Fury, in late February — has sent a deliberately mixed message about re-entering the renewed war. Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrich said Israel has 'no interest' in joining the US military campaign against Iran, Al Jazeera reported, a public signal of restraint, even as CNN reported that Israel is quietly readying plans in case it is drawn back in. Israel has stayed off the battlefield since the fighting reignited on 8 July, leaving the current phase a US-Iran duel, but has repeatedly warned it will respond 'with full force' if Iranian weapons strike its territory.
stories.explainer: Israel rejoining is the fastest way to widen the war, because it doubles the fronts Iran must fight and risks reactivating Hezbollah and other 'Axis of Resistance' militias along Israel's borders. Smotrich's 'no interest' and CNN's 'readying plans' are not contradictory: they describe a government that wants to stay out but is hedging against a single errant Iranian missile that could trip its 'full force' tripwire and force it back in.

stories.title: Iran's reprisals keep falling on US Gulf allies
stories.summary: Iran's retaliation has continued to fall on the US-aligned states that ring it rather than on American soil directly, with fresh reprisals against Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan accompanying the latest nights of strikes, the Guardian and Al Jazeera reported. Iran has separately claimed it attacked Amazon infrastructure in Bahrain — a rare claimed strike on commercial cloud-computing facilities — and in recent days a fire hit a Kuwaiti electricity-and-water plant. The pattern matches Iran's threat, reported by The Hill, that if US strikes persist 'no political border' will be safe, and its warning that it would answer US attacks by blacking out host countries. A leaked US assessment concluded Iran retained roughly 70% of its ballistic missiles after the spring campaign.
stories.explainer: Hitting the US through the Gulf states that shelter its bases raises the cost of the American campaign while pressuring those governments to lean on Washington for a ceasefire. A claimed strike on cloud infrastructure widens the target set from bases and power plants to the digital backbone of the region — a way to make hosting American forces and companies politically and economically unbearable.

notes: Evening refresh of the 2026-07-22 briefing (previous cut generated 11:08Z). The manual RSS refresh via scripts/fetch-news.mjs was again blocked by the environment's egress policy (403 on all news hosts), so this draws on the workflow-committed feed (data/news.json, generated 2026-07-22T18:34Z; freshest item 18:01Z). Since the morning briefing the war added an explicit escalation rung: President Trump declared the US would destroy an Iranian bridge or power plant for every Iranian attack in the Strait of Hormuz, and an Iranian official warned Tehran would answer by blacking out the Gulf states hosting US troops. Secretary of State Marco Rubio publicly judged Iran 'not serious about a deal,' dimming the US-mediated ceasefire talks and lifting oil; Jordan's army said it intercepted four of six Iranian missiles fired Wednesday; Iran's judiciary warned against 'infiltrators' and announced new executions under a post-12-day-war security law; and a new poll showed even Trump voters losing patience with the war while Trump said the midterms would not change his plan. The standing threats to strike 'Pickaxe Mountain' and Hegseth's ~$88bn Senate request ($37.5bn spent, ~$67bn to restock arms) remain live. Featured stories are ranked by systemic significance, scale, breadth, novelty and continuity; overview, keyPoints and stories describe the same events at different altitudes. Contested claims — the war's $37.5bn cost and ~$88bn ask, casualty and injury figures (17 dead, ~100 wounded), the '70%' missile-retention estimate, oil and pump prices, the depth and nature of 'Pickaxe Mountain,' Jordan's interception count, and Iranian- or Houthi-announced actions — are attributed to their sources.
